title: The Great Wife Hope

jokes.text: I Googled 'girls having fun,' and after wading through 97,000 pages of porn, I found 'crazy bowling.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks how he came up with the idea for crazy bowling
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific number and casual admission of porn-browsing
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness with a modern internet twist. The specificity of '97,000 pages' elevates it from generic porn joke

jokes.text: It's pretty sad when I can't pick up a guy on Super Nacho Tuesday.
jokes.setup: Empty bowling alley with no men
jokes.punchline: The idea that Super Nacho Tuesday is prime pickup territory
jokes.explanation: Decent observational humor with good economy, but fairly predictable setup

jokes.text: Come on, you pansies! I've seen fiercer fights in Parliament! / Yes, what is this, Prime Minister's Question Time?
jokes.setup: Calling fighters pansies and comparing to Parliament
jokes.punchline: The overly specific British parliamentary reference
jokes.explanation: Smart escalation from general Parliament to very specific PMQ reference, showing Simpsons' educational comedy

jokes.text: There's no better violence than self-inflicted violence.
jokes.setup: Watching MMA fighting
jokes.punchline: Homer's twisted philosophical observation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical dark humor that fits Homer's warped worldview

jokes.text: I'll remember this day on my deathbed. / Not everyone has a deathbed, son. You could die suddenly.
jokes.setup: Bart expressing gratitude for a special day
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual correction about different ways to die
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of a sentimental moment with Homer's matter-of-fact morbidity. Perfect character work.

jokes.text: Maybe some punk kid will shoot me to get into a gang. / Maybe.
jokes.setup: Discussion of sudden death
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual 'Maybe' to his son's violent death scenario
jokes.explanation: Homer's deadpan response to Bart's dark speculation is perfectly in character and economical

jokes.text: Just like the ancient Romans. / Yeah, except their empire was falling apart.
jokes.setup: Comparing MMA to gladiatorial combat
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual dismissal of Roman civilization
jokes.explanation: Decent historical irony but not particularly surprising for Simpsons standards

jokes.text: Stupid Romans.
jokes.setup: Bart points out Roman empire's collapse
jokes.punchline: Homer's childish response blaming the Romans for their own downfall
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - when confronted with historical complexity, he just gets petulant. Two words capture his entire worldview.

jokes.text: Filthy but obscure with a subtle scatological undertone.
jokes.setup: Asking for a swear word autograph
jokes.punchline: Bart's sophisticated description of profanity preferences
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart moment - his juvenile interests expressed with adult vocabulary sophistication

jokes.text: Sure, no problem. $25, please.
jokes.setup: Fighter agreeing to sign with elaborate swear word
jokes.punchline: Charging money for the autograph
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up expectation of accommodating fan request, reveals mercenary motive.

jokes.text: don't try this at home. Do it at the schoolyard. Someplace where, if you get hurt, we can sue not just them but the school, the county, the state, and that jackass Joe Biden.
jokes.setup: Standard parental advice about not trying dangerous stunts
jokes.punchline: Homer's litigious escalation ending with random Biden insult
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with Homer's twisted parental logic, though Biden reference feels somewhat random

jokes.text: Where's your brother? / I don't know, chasing ducks, buried alive? / He'll be missed. Let's move: I got to collect pine cones.
jokes.setup: Normal parental check on missing child
jokes.punchline: Marge's casual acceptance of 'buried alive' and immediate move to pine cone collecting
jokes.explanation: Dark humor subverting parental concern with Marge's matter-of-fact priorities

jokes.text: Die, die, die! / Just follow the dies, Mom.
jokes.setup: Bart apparently shouting violently
jokes.punchline: Lisa's clarification that he's giving directions
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection with economical wordplay - seems violent but is actually helpful

jokes.text: God didn't give you legs so you could use them as scissors.
jokes.setup: Bart using wrestling moves on another kid
jokes.punchline: Marge's creative anatomical theology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge line - practical parenting wisdom delivered with her characteristic earnest absurdity

jokes.text: 'Ultimate' makes everything worse. / Not Frisbee.
jokes.setup: Marge making a broad statement about 'Ultimate'
jokes.punchline: Lisa's immediate, specific counterexample
jokes.explanation: Lisa's logical precision undermining Marge's sweeping statement with perfect economy

jokes.text: Otto, don't you have a sack to hacky? / Indeed I do.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to get Otto to leave
jokes.punchline: Otto's earnest confirmation of his hacky sack obligations
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy with Otto's stoner earnestness, though the wordplay is fairly obvious

jokes.text: One... I messed up. One... I messed up. One... I messed up!
jokes.setup: Otto attempting to hacky sack
jokes.punchline: His repeated immediate failures
jokes.explanation: Predictable physical comedy but fits Otto's character well

jokes.text: You can't stop an entire sport. You'd need several other mothers, some sympathetic school officials and clergymen... A place to meet, some snacks... What else? A phone tree is invaluable. How you fixed for staplers?
jokes.setup: Marge wanting to stop Ultimate Punching
jokes.punchline: Nelson's detailed activism logistics ending with mundane office supplies
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character subversion - Nelson the bully as expert grassroots organizer. Perfect escalation from grand to mundane.

jokes.text: When I grow up, I hope to go into event planning. / Maybe someday you can do Lisa's wedding. / I'd like that.
jokes.setup: Nelson revealing his career aspirations
jokes.punchline: The sweet sincerity of the bully planning Lisa's wedding
jokes.explanation: Continuation of Nelson's character subversion with genuine sweetness that doesn't break character

jokes.text: Call me a killjoy, but I think that because this is not to my taste, no one else should be able to enjoy it.
jokes.setup: Standard protest against violent entertainment
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest admission of authoritarian impulse
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical honesty about moral crusading - character says the quiet part loud with perfect self-awareness

jokes.text: This sport is as fun for me as having kids is for you.
jokes.setup: Chett defending his sport to Marge
jokes.punchline: Comparing Ultimate Punching to the burden of parenthood
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever comparison that reframes the argument effectively

jokes.text: she got to you with a piece of Black Forest cake. / It wasn't just a piece, it was the whole cake! Frosting like snow on the eaves of a Bavarian castle!
jokes.setup: Bart accusing Homer of being bribed with cake
jokes.punchline: Homer's poetic confession about his cake weakness
jokes.explanation: Homer's unexpected eloquence about cake creates a perfect character moment of weakness and poetry

jokes.text: She knew my one weakness: that I'm weak!
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how Marge manipulated him
jokes.punchline: The circular logic of his weakness being weakness itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - tautological but somehow profound. Excellent economy and character integration.

jokes.text: One, two, we say boo! Three, four, stop the gore! Five, six, who's got tix? Seven, eight, use that gate.
jokes.setup: Protest chant against violence
jokes.punchline: The chant devolving into asking for tickets and directions
jokes.explanation: Decent satire of protest chants but fairly predictable in execution

jokes.text: I am not a scalper. I am a dude whose 200 friends did not show up.
jokes.setup: Being accused of ticket scalping
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and sad explanation
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection with pathetic specificity that makes the character sympathetic

jokes.text: Have you ever noticed how that baby of hers never says a thing? I think it saw something awful. / Me, too. That's exactly what it is. / What else could it be?
jokes.setup: Gossiping about Maggie
jokes.punchline: The immediate consensus on a baseless theory
jokes.explanation: Good satire of gossip logic but not particularly memorable

jokes.text: That woman has natural breasts.
jokes.setup: Men noticing Marge in the arena
jokes.punchline: The oddly clinical observation delivered as shocking news
jokes.explanation: Decent absurdist observation but relies somewhat on cheap shock value

jokes.text: Nobody tears down the septagon. Except for the arena crew because Hufflesnuffs on Ice needs the space tomorrow.
jokes.setup: Dramatic declaration about the septagon's permanence
jokes.punchline: Immediate practical contradiction with made-up ice show
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure with absurd ice show name that deflates the drama perfectly

jokes.text: When you yell like that, no one can hear me.
jokes.setup: Crowd cheering for the commissioner
jokes.punchline: His complaint about being drowned out by support
jokes.explanation: Decent ironic observation but not particularly original

jokes.text: At least in other sports, they're trying to put a ball in a net or a zone, but in this sport they don't put anything in anything, and if they did, I wouldn't want to see it.
jokes.setup: Marge criticizing Ultimate Punching
jokes.punchline: The accidental sexual innuendo in her criticism
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge accidentally creating innuendo while trying to be proper - good character integration

jokes.text: What's wrong, Marge? Do you have to ask your husband? Good one! Zing!
jokes.setup: Chett challenging Marge
jokes.punchline: The crowd's enthusiastic support for a weak insult
jokes.explanation: Adequate satire of crowd mentality but predictable execution

jokes.text: That flattens my soda pop. You're on.
jokes.setup: Marge being challenged to a fight
jokes.punchline: Her weirdly innocent euphemism for anger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character moment - expressing anger in the most wholesome way possible

jokes.text: he'll knock your block off your beautiful block
jokes.setup: Homer warning Marge about the fight
jokes.punchline: His attempt at romantic reassurance mid-threat
jokes.explanation: Good Homer character moment mixing concern with clumsy romance

jokes.text: If Gandhi could go without eating for a whole three-hour movie, I can do this.
jokes.setup: Marge drawing inspiration from Gandhi
jokes.punchline: Confusing his real-life fasting with watching the movie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that reveals Marge's innocent confusion about historical vs. cinematic experience. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Besides, I'm a woman. He'll probably go easy on me. / Or kick the crap out of you.
jokes.setup: Marge's optimistic assumption about chivalry
jokes.punchline: Bart's blunt reality check
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Marge's naive optimism and Bart's harsh realism. Effective misdirection structure.

jokes.text: Watch your language, moron.
jokes.setup: Someone using mild profanity
jokes.punchline: Homer's hypocritical language policing while insulting
jokes.explanation: Good ironic character comedy with Homer's oblivious hypocrisy

jokes.text: If I'm going to open a can of whup-tushy, I'd better get in shape with some rhythmic gymnastics.
jokes.setup: Marge preparing for a fight
jokes.punchline: Her wholesome euphemism and choice of least violent training
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character work - trying to be tough while remaining wholesome

jokes.text: This goes against every feminist bone in my body, but Dad, can't you control your woman?
jokes.setup: Lisa desperate to stop Marge from fighting
jokes.punchline: Her self-aware abandonment of her principles
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Lisa's desperation overriding her ideological consistency

jokes.text: One unlucky punch and Marge could be bedridden for life. Unable to move anything but her left toe. Yeah. 'FEED ME'
jokes.setup: Carl describing potential injury
jokes.punchline: Homer's fantasy of Marge spelling out food requests with her toe
jokes.explanation: Dark Homer humor turning disability into a feeding opportunity - perfectly selfish character work

jokes.text: You think just 'cause I'm black I know all other black people? / Well, I, uh, uh... / Actually, Drederick and I are very good friends. We met through Dr. Hibbert at a party at Bleeding Gums Murphy's house.
jokes.setup: Carl calling out racial assumption
jokes.punchline: The assumption turning out to be completely correct
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing with stereotype assumptions - sets up the trap then springs it perfectly

jokes.text: I'm always happy to impart my pugilistic prowess to a punching parvenu.
jokes.setup: Drederick offering to train Marge
jokes.punchline: His overly alliterative, intellectual boxing speak
jokes.explanation: Decent character work with the educated boxer trope but fairly predictable wordplay

jokes.text: I'd like to order 2 gazelles, an impala, and throw in a couple of wildebeests. Oh, and a two-liter bottle of diet cherry cola. You just have one-liter bottles? Okay, cancel the whole order.
jokes.setup: Drederick ordering exotic meat for his lions
jokes.punchline: Canceling the entire African safari order over soda size
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure showing absurd priorities - exotic animal order derailed by beverage preferences

jokes.text: Sorry, my English is not so good. I mean, he feels only pain.
jokes.setup: Akira saying Homer feels no pain after training
jokes.punchline: Correcting to the exact opposite meaning
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect economy - language barrier creating dark comedy

jokes.text: Back when I wrestled at Yale, I was in the lightest weight category: 'ether weight.'
jokes.setup: Frink talking about his wrestling past
jokes.punchline: The scientific pun on 'featherweight'
jokes.explanation: Good character-appropriate pun that fits Frink's scientific background perfectly

jokes.text: Now, get ready to face the wrath of the 'New Haven Nuisance!'
jokes.setup: Burns announcing his wrestling persona
jokes.punchline: Mild alliterative nickname that sounds more annoying than intimidating
jokes.explanation: Good character humor with Burns' wrestling name being appropriately weak and irritating rather than intimidating.

jokes.text: In order to save money, the following presidents will no longer be taught: Buchanan, Fillmore, Pierce, Bush, Bush...
jokes.setup: Budget cuts affecting curriculum
jokes.punchline: Cutting obscure presidents but including both Bushes
jokes.explanation: Decent educational satire with mild political edge but fairly predictable

jokes.text: More like 'Beat, Slay, Shove!'
jokes.setup: Marge reading 'Eat, Pray, Love'
jokes.punchline: Chett's violent reinterpretation
jokes.explanation: Obvious wordplay on popular culture reference - functional but uninspired

jokes.text: No can do. We're out of fireworks. / Why'd you shoot 'em off now? / I have trouble concentrating. I should be on antidepressants.
jokes.setup: Missing fireworks for dramatic effect
jokes.punchline: Honest admission about mental health issues
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from practical problem to personal revelation

jokes.text: Is there someone in there? / It's my own fault. I looked them in the eye when I gave them my lunch money.
jokes.setup: Someone asking about person in punching bag
jokes.punchline: Milhouse blaming himself for making eye contact during robbery
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse victim mentality - blaming himself for bullying. Dark humor about childhood trauma with good character integration.

jokes.text: It's my own fault. I looked them in the eye when I gave them my lunch money.
jokes.setup: Milhouse trapped in punching bag
jokes.punchline: Blaming himself for making eye contact during bullying
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse victim mentality - blaming himself for basic human behavior during abuse

jokes.text: Dad, I keep telling you, that won't work. / Let's go, Marge. My class reunion starts in an hour. / Uh, Moe, I have a confession to make. I'm just Homer dressed as Marge.
jokes.setup: Homer's bad plan revealed
jokes.punchline: His honest confession about the obvious disguise
jokes.explanation: Decent Homer absurdity but the drag gag is fairly predictable

jokes.text: Yeah, I thought a noisy public place would be the best spot to tell you that I've been dating your wife. / What?! / Also, could you tell her I'm dumping her?
jokes.setup: Krusty making a casual social invitation
jokes.punchline: Using the meetup to deliver devastating personal news
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and perfectly selfish Krusty behavior - using friendship for convenience

jokes.text: Hey, Bart, did you know that's a haiku?
jokes.setup: Bart giving advice about fighting dirty
jokes.punchline: Lisa recognizing the accidental poetic structure
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa character moment finding poetry in Bart's crudeness

jokes.text: And four obtuse angles over, weighing in at 115 pounds, Chett Englebrecht!
jokes.setup: Ring announcer describing the septagon
jokes.punchline: Using mathematical terminology instead of 'corners'
jokes.explanation: Decent mathematical humor but somewhat forced

jokes.text: That's the first time I ever hit a woman I didn't love.
jokes.setup: Chett apologizing after hitting Marge
jokes.punchline: The implication about his history of domestic violence
jokes.explanation: Dark misdirection that reveals disturbing character history with perfect economy

jokes.text: I'm gonna knock you out and moon your corpse.
jokes.setup: Bart defending his mother
jokes.punchline: His childishly specific threat escalation
jokes.explanation: Good Bart character work mixing tough talk with juvenile behavior

jokes.text: There sure is a lot o' talkin' for a professional fight. / That's what you get when you fight a woman. / And they spend all day eating bonbons and watching General Hospital. Which, by the way, has never been better.
jokes.setup: Complaining about too much talking in the fight
jokes.punchline: The sexist comment undermined by soap opera enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Good escalation undermining masculinity with soap opera knowledge

jokes.text: That's what you get when you fight a woman. / And they spend all day eating bonbons and watching General Hospital. Which, by the way, has never been better.
jokes.setup: Someone complains about too much talking during the fight
jokes.punchline: The sexist complaint turns into a genuine soap opera endorsement
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from sexist stereotyping to unexpected entertainment industry insight. The soap opera knowledge undermines the masculine posturing.

jokes.text: We can enjoy some mixed 'marital' arts. / Oh, no holds barred.
jokes.setup: Homer making a pun on martial arts
jokes.punchline: Marge's enthusiastic response with wrestling terminology
jokes.explanation: Predictable sexual pun but Marge's enthusiasm adds character depth

jokes.text: I don't know when we'll be in a septagon again. Want to settle this bad blood that's been going on between us since you were born?
jokes.setup: Bart suggesting a sibling fight
jokes.punchline: Claiming the rivalry started at Lisa's birth
jokes.explanation: Perfect sibling rivalry humor with Bart's characteristically petty long memory

jokes.text: I don't know when we'll be in a septagon again. Want to settle this bad blood that's been going on between us since you were born? / You're on.
jokes.setup: Bart suggests they fight while in the fighting venue
jokes.punchline: He frames their normal sibling rivalry as longstanding 'bad blood' requiring resolution
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that frames normal sibling dynamics in fight terminology. Bart's dramatic 'since you were born' treats ordinary sibling rivalry as epic feud.

tmdb_overview: The men and boys in Springfield become obsessed with Ultimate Punch Kick and Choke Championships, a violent sport where fighters inflict severe pain on each other. When Marge and her friends stage a protest against the sport, the show's promoter embraces the controversy and challenges Marge to a match, promising to shut down the competition if she wins. She begins to train with rhythmic gymnastics, but her family step in and get her professional martial arts help to give her a real chance at winning.
